title: Marriage and Family Adaptive Exam
description: 100 single-choice Marriage and Family questions. A normal round serves 25; the final exam serves all 100. Every distractor is a real course term or definition, so choose the best exact match.

questions.section: Foundations, Forms, Kinship, Theory, Methods, Class
questions.prompt: Which answer is the most precise exam meaning of "Family development / Life course theory"?

options.text: Analyzes the predictable stages families pass through over time: marriage, childbearing, launching children, the empty nest.
A family formed when one or both partners bring children from a prior relationship into a new marriage.
A family in which partners share power and decision-making roughly equally.

Views the family through power, inequality, and competition for resources. Arrangements persist because they serve the powerful — NOT because they benefit everyone. It critiques hierarchy rather than endorsing it.

questions.explanation: Best match: Family development / Life course theory - Analyzes the predictable stages families pass through over time: marriage, childbearing, launching children, the empty nest. The other choices are real Marriage and Family terms or definitions (Blended family / Stepfamily, Egalitarian family, Conflict theory), but they answer a neighboring concept rather than this exact prompt. Watch the general word(s): theory = theory means a formal lens for what to look for first; family = family means a specific membership or responsibility structure.
memoryFocus: theory means a formal lens for what to look for first
family means a specific membership or responsibility structure

questions.section: Foundations, Forms, Kinship, Theory, Methods, Class
questions.prompt: Which answer is the most precise exam meaning of "Instrumental vs. expressive roles"?

options.text: Views the family as a system whose parts perform functions that keep society stable — socializing children, regulating sex, providing emotional support. Key: roles exist because they help the system work.
Examines how gender inequality is created and reproduced in families — especially women's unequal share of power and unpaid labor.
Parsons' claim that families specialize: an instrumental role (breadwinner / task-leader) and an expressive role (nurturer / emotional caretaker).
Focuses on the shared meanings, symbols, and definitions people build through everyday interaction, and how these shape roles and the self.

questions.explanation: Best match: Instrumental vs. expressive roles - Parsons' claim that families specialize: an instrumental role (breadwinner / task-leader) and an expressive role (nurturer / emotional caretaker). The other choices are real Marriage and Family terms or definitions (Structural-functionalism, Feminist perspective, Symbolic interactionism), but they answer a neighboring concept rather than this exact prompt.
